stem: Let \( \mathbf{u} = ([[u1]], [[u2]], [[u3]]) \) and \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]], [[v2]], [[v3]]) \) in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \). The orthogonal projection of \( \mathbf{v} \) onto the line spanned by \( \mathbf{u} \) equals \( c\,\mathbf{u} \) for a scalar \( c \). What is \( c \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the dot product \( \mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{u} \) alone, without dividing by \( \mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{u} \).
This divides by \( \mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v} \) instead of \( \mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{u} \).
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct scalar.
This is off by one from the correct scalar.
This doubles the projection scalar.
This is off by two from the correct scalar.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{u} = ([[u1]], [[u2]], [[u3]]) \) and \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]], [[v2]], [[v3]]) \) in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \), and let \( L \) be the line through the origin spanned by \( \mathbf{u} \). What is the SQUARE of the distance from \( \mathbf{v} \) to its nearest point on \( L \)?
solution: The nearest point on \( L \) is the projection \( \operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf{u}}(\mathbf{v}) \), and by the Pythagorean theorem the squared distance is \( \|\mathbf{v}\|^2 - \dfrac{(\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{u})^2}{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{u}} = \dfrac{([[uu]])([[vv]]) - ([[dotvu]])^2}{[[uu]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \|\mathbf{v}\|^2 \) with no projection subtracted at all.
This is the projection SCALAR \( c \), not a squared distance.
This is \( |\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf{u}}(\mathbf{v})|^2 \) itself, the wrong quantity (it should be subtracted from \( \|\mathbf{v}\|^2 \), not reported alone).
This subtracts the projection scalar \( c \) instead of \( c^2\|\mathbf{u}\|^2 \).
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct squared distance.
This is off by one from the correct squared distance.
This doubles the squared distance.

stem: Let \( W \) be the subspace of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) spanned by the two ORTHOGONAL vectors \( \mathbf{w}_1 = ([[a]], [[b]], [[c]]) \) and \( \mathbf{w}_2 = ([[b]], [[negA]], 0) \), and let \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]], [[v2]], [[v3]]) \). The orthogonal projection of \( \mathbf{v} \) onto \( W \) is \( \operatorname{proj}_W(\mathbf{v}) = c_1\mathbf{w}_1 + c_2\mathbf{w}_2 \) where \( c_1 = \dfrac{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{w}_1}{\mathbf{w}_1\cdot\mathbf{w}_1} \) and \( c_2 = \dfrac{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{w}_2}{\mathbf{w}_2\cdot\mathbf{w}_2} \). What is the first coordinate of \( \operatorname{proj}_W(\mathbf{v}) \)?
solution: Compute \( c_1 = \dfrac{[[dot1]]}{[[w1w1]]} \) and \( c_2 = \dfrac{[[dot2]]}{[[w2w2]]} \); the first coordinate of \( c_1\mathbf{w}_1 + c_2\mathbf{w}_2 \) is \( c_1\cdot[[a]] + c_2\cdot[[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the scalars \( c_1 \) and \( c_2 \) directly, forgetting to multiply each by the matching basis coordinate.
This keeps only the \( \mathbf{w}_1 \) component of the projection, dropping the \( \mathbf{w}_2 \) term entirely.
This keeps only the \( \mathbf{w}_2 \) component of the projection, dropping the \( \mathbf{w}_1 \) term entirely.
This returns \( \mathbf{v} \)'s own first coordinate, as if no projection were applied.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.

stem: Let \( W \) be the subspace of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) spanned by the two orthogonal vectors \( \mathbf{w}_1 = ([[a]], [[b]], [[c]]) \) and \( \mathbf{w}_2 = ([[b]], [[negA]], 0) \), and let \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]], [[v2]], [[v3]]) \). What is the SQUARE of the distance from \( \mathbf{v} \) to its nearest point in \( W \)?
solution: By the Pythagorean theorem, \( \operatorname{dist}(\mathbf{v},W)^2 = \|\mathbf{v}\|^2 - c_1^2\|\mathbf{w}_1\|^2 - c_2^2\|\mathbf{w}_2\|^2 = [[vv]] - \dfrac{([[dot1]])^2}{[[w1w1]]} - \dfrac{([[dot2]])^2}{[[w2w2]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract the projection's squared length, giving \( \|\mathbf{v}\|^2 \) itself.
This is \( \|\operatorname{proj}_W(\mathbf{v})\|^2 \), the wrong quantity (it must be subtracted from \( \|\mathbf{v}\|^2 \)).
This subtracts only the \( \mathbf{w}_1 \) component's contribution, forgetting \( \mathbf{w}_2 \).
This subtracts only the \( \mathbf{w}_2 \) component's contribution, forgetting \( \mathbf{w}_1 \).
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct squared distance.
This is off by one from the correct squared distance.

stem: The plane \( P \) through the origin in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) has equation \( [[a]]x + [[b]]y + [[c]]z = 0 \), so \( \mathbf{n} = ([[a]], [[b]], [[c]]) \) is normal to \( P \). Let \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]], [[v2]], [[v3]]) \). What is the SQUARE of the distance from \( \mathbf{v} \) to its nearest point in \( P \)?
solution: The distance from \( \mathbf{v} \) to the plane \( \mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{x}=0 \) is \( \dfrac{|\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{n}|}{\|\mathbf{n}\|} \), so the squared distance is \( \dfrac{(\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{n})^2}{\|\mathbf{n}\|^2} = \dfrac{([[dotnv]])^2}{[[nn]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the raw dot product \( \mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{n} \), neither squared nor divided by \( \|\mathbf{n}\|^2 \).
This squares the dot product but forgets to divide by \( \|\mathbf{n}\|^2 \).
This is \( \|\mathbf{v}\|^2 \), unrelated to the distance to the plane.
This forgets to square the dot product before dividing.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct squared distance.
This is off by one from the correct squared distance.
